title: Doubly Linked List
description: Adding a prev reference to each Node, enabling backward traversal and O(1) removal given a direct node reference -- the manual version of what java.util.LinkedList actually is.
objectives: Add a prev reference to Node, enabling backward traversal
Insert and delete a node while maintaining both prev and next links
Understand the extra bookkeeping cost vs the benefits gained

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Singly Linked Lists Only Go Forward

In the Singly Linked List lesson, current.next was the only way to move -- there was no way to go BACKWARD from a node. A doubly linked list adds a second reference, prev, pointing to the previous node.
A linked list where each Node holds references to both the next node AND the previous node, allowing traversal in either direction.
2. The Node Class, Now With prev

Every link now needs to be set in BOTH directions manually -- second.prev = first must be set alongside first.next = second, or the backward chain breaks even though the forward chain looks fine. This doubled bookkeeping is the trade-off for gaining backward traversal.
3. Insert at Head, Maintaining Both Links

if (head != null) { head.prev = newNode; } is essential -- without this check, if the list was empty (head == null), head.prev would throw a NullPointerException (recall Exception Handling's checked/unchecked distinction: this is an unchecked exception, easy to trigger accidentally here).
4. Deleting a Node: The Real Benefit

sections.title: Why this deletion is O(1) given a direct node reference
points: node.prev.next = node.next relinks the PREVIOUS node past the one being deleted
node.next.prev = node.prev relinks the NEXT node's backward pointer past it too
No WALKING the list required, unlike a singly linked list's delete() from the previous lesson, which had to search forward from head to find the node just before the target
This only works because we already HAVE a direct reference to the node (middleNode) -- finding a node by value still requires a search either way

sections.title: Singly vs doubly linked list
rows: Delete given a direct node reference
O(n) -- must find the PREVIOUS node by walking from head

sections.text: This is the payoff for List: ArrayList vs LinkedList's earlier claim that LinkedList has fast middle insertion/removal -- java.util.LinkedList is internally a doubly linked list exactly like the one built here, which is precisely why it can relink neighbors in O(1) once positioned there.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Updating next without also updating the corresponding prev (or vice versa), leaving the two directions inconsistent with each other.
Forgetting the null check before dereferencing head.prev (or node.prev/node.next) when the list might be empty or the node might be at an end.
Assuming deleteNode(node) is O(1) even when you only have the node's VALUE, not a direct reference -- finding it by value still requires an O(n) search first.
Confusing prev/next direction when relinking during deletion, accidentally creating a broken or circular chain.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the key structural difference between a singly and doubly linked list?
questions.answer: Each Node in a doubly linked list holds an additional prev reference to the previous node, alongside next, enabling traversal in both directions -- a singly linked list can only move forward.

questions.question: Why can a doubly linked list delete a node in O(1) given a direct reference to it, while a singly linked list cannot?
questions.answer: A doubly linked list already knows the previous node via node.prev, so relinking around the deleted node needs no search. A singly linked list has no prev reference, so it must walk from head to find the node just before the target before it can relink anything.

questions.question: What is the memory trade-off of a doubly linked list compared to a singly linked list?
questions.answer: Each node requires an extra reference (prev) beyond next, roughly doubling the per-node reference overhead, in exchange for backward traversal and O(1) deletion given a direct node reference.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A doubly linked list extends the singly linked list's Node with a prev reference alongside next, enabling backward traversal. This costs extra memory per node (two references instead of one) but gains real power: given a direct reference to a node, deletion is O(1), since node.prev.next and node.next.prev can be relinked immediately -- no walking the list to find the previous node, unlike a singly linked list. This is exactly why java.util.LinkedList, previewed in List: ArrayList vs LinkedList, is internally a doubly linked list.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Doubly linked list = a treasure hunt where each clue ALSO tells you where the PREVIOUS clue was. Extra bookkeeping (set both prev and next), but now you can walk backward, and deleting a KNOWN clue is instant -- no retracing your steps needed.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What extra reference does a doubly linked list's Node have compared to a singly linked list's?
Why is deletion O(1) in a doubly linked list, given a direct node reference?
What's the memory trade-off of adding prev to every node?
Which real Java class is internally implemented as a doubly linked list?

sections.title: Design Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Design your own implementation of a linked list, supporting get(index), addAtHead(val), addAtTail(val), addAtIndex(index, val), and deleteAtIndex(index).

examples.input: addAtHead(1); addAtTail(3); addAtIndex(1, 2); get(1); deleteAtIndex(1); get(1)
examples.explanation: After addAtHead(1) and addAtTail(3): [1,3]. addAtIndex(1,2) inserts 2 at index 1: [1,2,3]. get(1) returns 2. deleteAtIndex(1) removes it: [1,3]. get(1) now returns 3.

constraints: At most 2000 calls will be made
hints: Using a doubly linked list with both head and tail sentinel-style tracking makes addAtTail O(1) as well as addAtHead, unlike a plain singly linked list (recall Singly Linked List's O(n) insertAtTail).

approaches.label: Brute Force (Singly Linked List backing)
approaches.explanation: Back the design with a singly linked list -- simpler per-node structure, but addAtTail requires walking to the end every time.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(index) per operation -- addAtTail is O(n)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Back the design with a doubly linked list, tracking both head and tail directly -- addAtHead and addAtTail both become O(1), and deletion given a position is efficient in both directions.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(1) for addAtHead/addAtTail, O(index) for get/addAtIndex/deleteAtIndex

sections.title: LRU Cache
sections.problemStatement: Design a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache with a fixed capacity. It should support get(key) and put(key, value), both in O(1) average time. When the cache exceeds capacity, evict the LEAST recently used item.

examples.input: LRUCache(2); put(1,1); put(2,2); get(1); put(3,3); get(2); put(4,4); get(1); get(3); get(4)
examples.explanation: Capacity 2. Inserting a 3rd item evicts the least recently used one; accessing an item makes it most recently used.

constraints: At most 2*10^5 calls to get and put
hints: Combine a HashMap (for O(1) key lookup, from the Hashing lesson) with a doubly linked list (for O(1) reordering and eviction, from THIS lesson) -- the classic LRU cache design.
The doubly linked list's ORDER represents recency: move a node to the front on every access, and evict from the back when over capacity.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Use Java's own LinkedHashMap with access-order enabled -- it already implements LRU eviction internally, but hides the doubly-linked-list mechanics this lesson is teaching.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Manually built HashMap + doubly linked list with dummy head/tail sentinels -- shows exactly how LRU eviction works underneath, using this lesson's own Node structure.

sections.title: Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
sections.problemStatement: You are given a doubly linked list where nodes also have an optional CHILD pointer, possibly pointing to a separate doubly linked list. Flatten the list so all nodes appear in a single-level doubly linked list, following the order given by the multilevel structure.

examples.explanation: The child list under node 3 gets spliced in immediately after node 3, before continuing with node 4.

constraints: The number of nodes will not exceed 1000
hints: This is naturally a DFS (Graph DFS / Tree DFS pattern applied to lists): whenever a node has a child, recursively flatten that child list FIRST, then splice it in between the current node and its original next.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Iteratively use an explicit Stack (Stack and Monotonic Stack) to process child lists depth-first, without true recursion.
approaches.output: Flattened list in the documented DFS order
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the stack in the worst case (deeply nested children)

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: True recursion: flatten the child list first, splice it in, then continue with the rest of the current level -- directly mirrors the DFS pattern.
approaches.output: Flattened list in the documented DFS order
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(d) recursion depth, d = maximum nesting depth
